01a_quotes.title: The Uneven Impact of Generative AI on Entrepreneurial Performance: Evidence from a Field Experiment in Kenya

study_design: In a field experiment with Kenyan entrepreneurs, we evaluated the impact of AI advice on small business revenues and profits by randomizing access to a GPT-4-powered AI business assistant.

In this paper, we explore the potential of AI to impact firm performance in a randomized controlled trial (RCT) with 640 Kenyan small business entrepreneurs, half of whom were randomly assigned to receive access to a GPT-4-powered AI assistant over WhatsApp.

Following the third pre-treatment survey, participants were randomly assigned to either the treatment group—which received access to the generative AI-powered business assistant—or the control group, which received access to a set of publicly available business training guides, both of which are discussed in more detail below.
To improve the statistical precision of our experiment, we stratified random assignment by quartiles of pre-treatment business performance and by gender.
As expected under randomization, the two groups were comparable across a range of baseline characteristics and performance measures (Table A1).
task: Entrepreneurs could ask follow-up questions, explore alternatives, and learn more before deciding whether to implement advice.

Consistent with the many challenges facing the owners of young businesses, entrepreneurs used the AI assistant to discuss a wide range of topics. Conversations included how to motivate employees, allocate resources when expanding a store, adopt best practices in poultry farming, and manage bankruptcy.

Participants in the AI condition completed a short (5–10 minute) online survey-based training. This training explained that the chatbot they would be interacting with was an AI system (not a human), that the AI's information would not be up to date, and that the advice it provided could sometimes be inaccurate.

Participants assigned to the control group received access to a series of business training guides that were also sent over WhatsApp. These guides, developed by the International Labour Organization for SMB entrepreneurs in emerging markets, focused on strategies for business development and growth (ILO, 2014). Control group participants were sent links to three PDFs from a nine-part series, along with a link to access the remaining materials.
population: This recruitment and screening process produced a final sample of 640 Kenya SMB entrepreneurs.
The sample is geographically diverse: participants came from 44 of Kenya's 47 counties, with the largest shares from Nairobi (133), Kiambu (53), and Nakuru (47) (see Figure A1).
The median entrepreneur was 26 years old, and most had at least some college education, with only 10% reporting no college experience.
Women made up 32% of the sample, consistent with lower rates of female business ownership in Kenya (The World Bank Group, 2018).
The businesses represented in the sample ranged from fast food vendors to poultry farmers to cybershop owners (who provide services such as internet access, printing, scanning, and computer support).
The most common sectors in the sample were Food and Beverage (27%), Clothing (21%), and Agriculture (19%).
The median entrepreneur in the sample had been running their business for one year or less and had no employees.

Reflecting both the emerging-market context and these firms' limited operating histories, entrepreneurs reported modest pre-treatment business performance. The average pre-treatment monthly profit was about KSh 19,550, and reported monthly profits ranged from about KSh 2,650 at the 5th percentile to about KSh 55,100 at the 95th percentile.
arms: Following the third pre-treatment survey, participants were randomly assigned to either the treatment group—which received access to the generative AI-powered business assistant—or the control group, which received access to a set of publicly available business training guides, both of which are discussed in more detail below.

The generative AI-powered business assistant was built on GPT-4, a large language model released by OpenAI in March 2023 (OpenAI, 2023). To ensure accessibility, the AI assistant was deployed through WhatsApp, a messaging platform already widely familiar to participants in Kenya. Access was restricted so that the assistant would only respond to phone numbers belonging to participants in the treatment group, ensuring that participants in the control group could not use the tool.

Participants assigned to the control group received access to a series of business training guides that were also sent over WhatsApp. These guides, developed by the International Labour Organization for SMB entrepreneurs in emerging markets, focused on strategies for business development and growth (ILO, 2014). Control group participants were sent links to three PDFs from a nine-part series, along with a link to access the remaining materials.

Of the 640 study participants, 11 attritted from the AI-assistant group and 7 from the control group. The 1.2 percentage point difference in response rates between the two conditions is not statistically significant (p ≈ 0.35).
outcome_definitions: To measure firm performance, we follow approaches commonly used in emerging market contexts: collecting multiple self-reported performance measures in both pre-treatment and post-treatment survey waves (McKenzie, 2012), and constructing indices to reduce measurement noise (Kling, Liebman, and Katz, 2007).

Specifically, in each of the three pre-treatment and four post-treatment waves, we collected standard self-reported measures of weekly and monthly profits and revenue (De Mel, McKenzie, and Woodruff, 2009).

Our primary outcome is a business performance index that aggregates weekly and monthly profits and revenues. The goal of this composite index is to reduce the noise that is common in self-reported performance data, and such indices are widely used in survey-based empirical research (Kling, Liebman, and Katz, 2007) and in emerging markets (Campos et al., 2017; Anderson and McKenzie, 2022).

We constructed the standardized index following the procedure outlined in Kling, Liebman, and Katz (2007). First, we winsorized each of the four measures (at either the 99% or 95% level, depending on specification) to limit the influence of outliers. Second, we standardized each variable by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation of the control group's post-treatment outcomes, which puts all four index components on a common standardized scale. Finally, we averaged the four standardized components in each survey wave to create the index.

Comparing the average values of the performance index among treatment and control participants provides an aggregate measure of how the AI system impacts firm performance, measured in standard deviations. If the difference in the performance index between the AI assistant condition and the control group is greater than zero, it means that the AI system led to improvements in firm performance relative to control; if it is less than zero, it indicates a decline in business performance. The magnitude of these effects can be interpreted as standard deviations of performance improvement: for example, a treatment effect of 0.2 indicates that the AI system improved business performance by 0.2 standard deviations relative to the post-treatment control participants' performance distribution.
statistical_results: We find no statistically significant average treatment effect of access to the AI assistant on firm performance (winsorized at the 99% level) relative to control, as shown in Estimate A in Figure 3 and Table 1 (β = 0.04 standard deviations (sd), p = 0.34, 95% CI = [−0.05, 0.15]).
For low performers, access to the AI assistant reduced average business performance relative to the control group (β = −0.08 s.d., p = 0.01, 95% CI = [−0.13, −0.03]).
Multiplying the standard deviation by our −0.08 s.d. estimate suggests that the AI assistant caused a drop of 4,748 Shillings ($33.34) in revenue per month; a 9.2% relative decrease.
For high performers, we find suggestive evidence that AI assistance improved performance relative to control (β = 0.16 s.d., p = 0.07, 95% CI = [−0.02, 0.33]).

Multiplying the standard deviation of monthly revenue by our estimate, now a 0.16 s.d. increase, which suggests that AI caused an increase of 9,496 Shillings (USD $66.69) in monthly revenue; a 18% relative increase.
Initially, low-performing entrepreneurs experienced a 0.23 sd lower causal effect (p = 0.01, 95% CI = [−0.41, −0.05]) of the AI assistant compared to high performers.
In monetary terms, the estimated difference in effect size between the two groups represents a change in in the impact of AI on a firm's monthly revenue of roughly 13,651 Shillings (USD $95.86).

The observed pattern of results—a null average effect, which masks significant heterogeneity by initial business performance—holds when we winsorize our business performance outcome at the 95% instead of the 99% level and when we exclude participants who did not complete all post-treatment surveys. Across these specifications, our most conservative estimate of treatment effect heterogeneity is 0.18 s.d. (p < 0.05, 95% CI = [0.02, 0.34]).

Panel A shows that treated entrepreneurs are 6.3 percentage points more likely to reference working with or learning from the AI assistant (p < 0.0001, 95% CI = [3.336; 9.188]). This effect is concentrated among high performers, where the increase is 10.8 percentage points (p < 0.001), compared to a 1.8 point increase among low performers that is not statistically significant (p = 0.19), with the between-group difference statistically significant at p < 0.01.

Panel B provides direct evidence for our tailored implementation hypothesis: treated high performers use 20% more uncommon words than those in the control group (p < 0.05, 95% CI = [1.04; 1.36]), consistent with more specific, tailored changes.

Panel C supports our generic implementation hypothesis: treated low performers are significantly more likely to report price cuts or advertising. Among low performers, 39% in the control group mention these strategies, compared to 51% in the treated group (a difference of 11.8 percentage points, p < 0.05, 95% CI = [0.8775; 22.78]).

Model 1 shows that a one standard deviation increase in a given AI advice embedding element correlates with a 0.26 standard deviation increase in the corresponding business changes embedding element (p < 0.0001).

With this control, a one standard deviation increase in an AI advice embedding dimension leads to a 0.127 standard deviation increase in the same dimension for subsequent business changes (p < 0.0001, 95% CI = [0.1073, 0.1475]).
We find no evidence that low and high performers differ in their likelihood of following AI advice.

Model 3 in Table 3 predicts whether an entrepreneur is a low or high performer based solely on their business changes text, achieving 58.4% accuracy—significantly better than the 50.5% no-information benchmark (p = 0.012, 95% CI = [0.52, 0.65]).
Model 4 shows we can also predict treatment status with 59.8% accuracy (p = 0.004, 95% CI = [0.53, 0.66]), indicating that treated entrepreneurs make different changes than control entrepreneurs.
Model 5 simultaneously predicts both performance level and treatment status (four categories total), achieving 33.8% accuracy compared to a 25.4% baseline (p = 0.004, 95% CI = [0.27, 0.41]).

Table 3 shows that our model achieves an accuracy of only 43.2% on the test set (p = 0.97, 95% CI = [0.34, 0.53]), which is not statistically distinguishable from the "no information" random-guess benchmark of 51.4% accuracy.
The model achieves an accuracy of only 51.8% (Table 3; p = 0.54, 95% CI = [0.42, 0.61]), which is exactly the same as the "no information" random-guess benchmark of 51.8%.

Panel A of Table A5 shows that the two groups have a similar likelihood of sending at least one message (88.2% for low vs. 84.2% for high performers), ask similar numbers of questions (18.6 vs. 15.5 on average), pose a similar number of numeric follow-ups (5.34 vs. 4.57), and write questions of similar length (13.5 vs. 14.4 words).

Panel B of Table A5 shows that low and high performers receive an average of 21.5 and 25.6 pieces of advice, respectively. The median number of suggestions is also similar: 13 for low performers and 11 for high performers. Additionally, the average length of AI responses is nearly identical (168 words for low performers, 166 words for high performers).

We collected a total of 4,434 performance observations for each of the four business performance index components, of which 2,226 were from the AI-assistant condition and 2,208 were from the control group (summary statistics for the outcome variables are provided in Table A2).
The average pre-treatment monthly profit was about KSh 19,550, and reported monthly profits ranged from about KSh 2,650 at the 5th percentile to about KSh 55,100 at the 95th percentile.
Table A2 shows average monthly revenues are 51,392 Kenyan Shillings (USD $360.90) with a standard deviation of 59,353 Shillings.
ambiguity_or_caveats: While we are unable to reject the null hypothesis of no average treatment effect on firm revenues and profits, we find that the effect for entrepreneurs who were low-performing at baseline is nearly 0.25 standard deviations lower than for initial high performers.

Our findings come from a single context (small business entrepreneurs in Kenya), cover a limited time span (5 months), and test only a specific implementation of AI assistance (a carefully prompt engineered GPT-4 instance).
In our context, even the highest-performing firms operate with relatively limited resources compared to businesses in developed economies.

Although the five-month duration of our experiment is considerably longer than nearly all prior experiments measuring the economic impacts of generative AI (cf. Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond, 2025), there may still be longer-term impacts we do not observe.

For instance, if low-performing firms are more likely to implement significant changes that require time to show positive effects, our results would understate benefits for this group. However, if the "generic" advice implemented by low performers (e.g., price cuts and advertising investments) proves counterproductive over longer horizons, our analysis would understate negative impacts.

Our experiment also took place from May to November 2023, when exposure to and experience with generative AI tools such as ChatGPT was still low, particularly in developing market contexts such as Kenya.

As business owners and workers become more familiar with generative AI tools, experiments such as ours may yield different results because treated participants, control participants, and even competing businesses outside the experiment may all be using AI tools.
Furthermore, our study evaluates an AI assistance built on GPT-4 with only a relatively short system prompt and that was distributed to entrepreneurs with 5-10 minutes of online training.

Although substantial testing and iteration went into the developing our system prompt, it is possible that fine-tuning the language model using a corpus of relevant information, further iterating on the system prompt, and/or providing more training to entrepreneurs interacting with the AI assistant could improve the suggested positive impacts among high performers and reduce (or reverse) the adverse effects among low performers.

Additionally, the AI assistant's heterogeneous impacts may be partially attributable to biases in GPT-4's training data, and a model trained on better data could yield positive returns to AI for even the most challenging business tasks (Tao et al., 2023).

While our abductive mechanism analysis is most consistent with variation in judgment and ability driving differences in the types of AI advice that is implemented, we cannot fully rule out alternative possibilities.

As with many studies in emerging markets (De Mel, McKenzie, and Woodruff, 2009), we also rely on self-reported survey data. As a consequence, our findings could partially reflect differences in how high and low performers respond to treatment in their reporting behavior, rather than actual business changes.

Although we attempted to mitigate some of these concerns by using a control group that received business training materials (rather than no intervention), we cannot completely rule out reporting-based explanations for our findings given the exploratory nature of our mechanism analyses.

Similarly, while our study's sample size of 640 entrepreneurs is comparable to other experiments with firms in emerging markets (McKenzie, 2021), like these other studies, we still face challenges when it comes to statistical power (McKenzie, 2025).

As a consequence, while high performers appear to make more tailored business changes and might have benefited from using the AI, we cannot strongly rule out the possibility that these patterns could result from sampling variability.
Scholars have speculated that limited training data from emerging markets may cause LLMs to provide less useful or erroneous advice in such settings (Björkegren, 2023; Tao et al., 2023).

Because existing evidence has found that delivering static training content over text messages has little effect on firm performance, we interpret this intervention as a placebo control condition (Cusolito, Darova, and McKenzie, 2023; Mehmood, 2023; Davies et al., 2024).

01a_quotes.notes: This is a randomized controlled trial (RCT) field experiment conducted in Kenya with 640 small business entrepreneurs. The study tests the impact of access to a GPT-4-powered AI business assistant delivered via WhatsApp on firm performance (measured through self-reported revenues and profits). The main finding is a null average treatment effect, but with substantial heterogeneity: low-performing entrepreneurs experienced approximately 10% worse performance, while high-performing entrepreneurs experienced approximately 15% better performance. The paper includes extensive mechanism analysis using text embeddings and machine learning to understand why low and high performers experienced different effects. The authors find that low performers tended to implement generic advice (price cuts, advertising) while high performers implemented more tailored, business-specific changes. The experiment ran from May to November 2023, with 5 months of post-treatment follow-up.

01_extraction.title: The Uneven Impact of Generative AI on Entrepreneurial Performance: Evidence from a Field Experiment in Kenya
01_extraction.study_design_description: A randomized controlled trial (RCT) with 640 Kenyan small business entrepreneurs, half of whom were randomly assigned to receive access to a GPT-4-powered AI assistant over WhatsApp. The other half received access to a set of publicly available business training guides. Stratified random assignment was used by quartiles of pre-treatment business performance and by gender. The experiment ran from May to November 2023, with three pre-treatment survey waves and four post-treatment survey waves.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Entrepreneurs in the treatment group could interact with a GPT-4-powered AI business assistant deployed through WhatsApp to discuss a wide range of business topics including how to motivate employees, allocate resources when expanding a store, adopt best practices in poultry farming, and manage bankruptcy. Entrepreneurs could ask follow-up questions, explore alternatives, and learn more before deciding whether to implement advice. Control group participants received access to a series of business training guides developed by the International Labour Organization for SMB entrepreneurs in emerging markets, focused on strategies for business development and growth.
01_extraction.population_description: Final sample of 640 Kenya SMB entrepreneurs, geographically diverse across 44 of Kenya's 47 counties. The median entrepreneur was 26 years old with at least some college education (only 10% reporting no college experience). Women made up 32% of the sample. Businesses ranged from fast food vendors to poultry farmers to cybershop owners, with the most common sectors being Food and Beverage (27%), Clothing (21%), and Agriculture (19%). The median entrepreneur had been running their business for one year or less and had no employees. Average pre-treatment monthly profit was about KSh 19,550, with reported monthly profits ranging from about KSh 2,650 at the 5th percentile to about KSh 55,100 at the 95th percentile.

arms.description: Received access to a generative AI-powered business assistant built on GPT-4, deployed through WhatsApp. Access was restricted so that the assistant would only respond to phone numbers belonging to participants in the treatment group. Participants completed a short (5–10 minute) online survey-based training explaining that the chatbot was an AI system (not a human), that the AI's information would not be up to date, and that the advice it provided could sometimes be inaccurate.

Received access to a series of business training guides developed by the International Labour Organization for SMB entrepreneurs in emerging markets, focused on strategies for business development and growth. Control group participants were sent links to three PDFs from a nine-part series, along with a link to access the remaining materials, all sent over WhatsApp.

outcomes.outcome_description: A composite standardized index aggregating weekly and monthly profits and revenues. Constructed by winsorizing each of the four measures (at either the 99% or 95% level), standardizing each variable by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation of the control group's post-treatment outcomes, and averaging the four standardized components. The index is measured in standard deviations of the control group's post-treatment performance distribution.
Self-reported monthly revenue collected in pre-treatment and post-treatment survey waves.
Self-reported monthly profit collected in pre-treatment and post-treatment survey waves.
Whether treated entrepreneurs reference working with or learning from the AI assistant in post-treatment surveys.
Use of uncommon words in business changes text, measured as a ratio comparing treated entrepreneurs to control group.
Whether entrepreneurs report price cuts or advertising as business strategies.
Correlation between AI advice embedding elements and corresponding business changes embedding elements, measured via text embeddings.
Machine learning model accuracy in predicting whether an entrepreneur is a low or high performer based solely on their business changes text.
Machine learning model accuracy in predicting treatment status based on business changes text.
Machine learning model accuracy in simultaneously predicting both performance level and treatment status (four categories total).

For low performers, access to the AI assistant reduced average business performance relative to the control group (β = −0.08 s.d., p = 0.01, 95% CI = [−0.13, −0.03]).
Multiplying the standard deviation by our −0.08 s.d. estimate suggests that the AI assistant caused a drop of 4,748 Shillings ($33.34) in revenue per month; a 9.2% relative decrease.

01_extraction.notes: Attrition was minimal and balanced: 11 from AI-assistant group and 7 from control group (1.2 percentage point difference, p ≈ 0.35). The study collected 4,434 total performance observations (2,226 from treatment, 2,208 from control). Average monthly revenue was 51,392 Kenyan Shillings (USD $360.90) with SD of 59,353 Shillings. The paper reports no evidence that low and high performers differ in their likelihood of following AI advice. The authors note several important caveats: (1) findings are from a single context (Kenya) over a limited time span (5 months) testing a specific GPT-4 implementation; (2) the experiment occurred May–November 2023 when exposure to generative AI was still low in developing markets; (3) the AI assistant used only a relatively short system prompt and entrepreneurs received only 5–10 minutes of training; (4) results rely on self-reported survey data, so findings could partially reflect differences in reporting behavior rather than actual business changes; (5) sample size of 640 is comparable to other emerging-market firm experiments but still faces statistical power challenges; (6) the control condition (static training content via text messages) is interpreted as a placebo control based on prior evidence that such interventions have little effect on firm performance. The paper's mechanism analysis uses text embeddings and machine learning to infer why low and high performers experienced different effects, but the authors acknowledge they cannot fully rule out alternative explanations.

02_method.rationale: The study explicitly describes itself as 'A randomized controlled trial (RCT)' with participants 'randomly assigned to receive access to a GPT-4-powered AI assistant' versus business training guides. This is a randomized experiment conducted in a real-world setting with actual entrepreneurs running their businesses, but it is not a workplace field experiment in the traditional sense—it's a randomized intervention delivered remotely via WhatsApp to geographically dispersed small business owners.

per_outcome.operationalization: Composite index averaging four standardized measures (weekly/monthly profits and revenues), winsorized and standardized to control group post-treatment distribution
Self-reported monthly revenue in Kenyan Shillings collected via pre-treatment and post-treatment surveys
Self-reported monthly profit in Kenyan Shillings collected via pre-treatment and post-treatment surveys
Percentage of treated entrepreneurs who mention working with or learning from the AI assistant in post-treatment surveys
Ratio of uncommon word usage in business changes text comparing treated entrepreneurs to control group
Percentage of entrepreneurs reporting price cuts or advertising as business strategies in surveys
Correlation between text embeddings of AI advice elements and business changes elements, measured in standard deviations
Machine learning model accuracy predicting low vs. high performer status from business changes text alone
Machine learning model accuracy predicting treatment vs. control status from business changes text
Machine learning model accuracy predicting both performance level and treatment status simultaneously (four categories)

04_onet.rationale: The participants are SMB entrepreneurs running their own businesses across diverse sectors (food, clothing, agriculture, etc.), making strategic decisions about 'how to motivate employees, allocate resources when expanding a store, adopt best practices...and manage bankruptcy.' These are the core responsibilities of general and operations managers. While the businesses are small (median has no employees), the entrepreneurs are performing the full range of managerial work—planning, organizing, directing, and controlling business operations—rather than executing a narrow technical task. The study measures their productivity as business owners doing managerial work, not as workers performing a specific occupational activity.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: The study has two arms: a control group receiving business training guides and a treatment group receiving access to a GPT-4-powered AI business assistant. The AI arm is the treatment condition.
llm_extracted.notes: The primary outcome is a standardized business performance index aggregating weekly and monthly profits and revenues. Figure 3 and Table 1 report the average treatment effect as β = 0.04 standard deviations (p = 0.34, 95% CI = [-0.05, 0.15]). The paper states 'we are unable to reject the null hypothesis of no average treatment effect on firm revenues and profits' (page 3). The study used stratified randomization by quartiles of pre-treatment business performance and by gender, with 640 total participants (320 per arm). The effect size of 0.04 s.d. is interpreted as a standardized effect (Cohen's d). The paper reports heterogeneous effects by baseline performance: low performers had β = -0.08 s.d. (p = 0.01), high performers had β = 0.16 s.d. (p = 0.07), with a heterogeneous treatment effect of β = 0.23 s.d. (p = 0.01) between the two groups.
source_quotes: We are unable to reject the null hypothesis that, on average, access to generative AI had no effect on business performance relative to the control, as shown in Estimate A in Figure 3 and Table 1 (β = 0.04 standard deviations (sd), p = 0.34, 95% CI = [−0.05, 0.15]).
This recruitment and screening process produced a final sample of 640 Kenya SMB entrepreneurs.

Following the third pre-treatment survey, participants were randomly assigned to either the treatment group—which received access to the generative AI-powered business assistant—or the control group, which received access to a set of publicly available business training guides
Our primary outcome is a business performance index that aggregates weekly and monthly profits and revenues.

Comparing the average values of the performance index among treatment and control participants provides an aggregate measure of how the AI system impacts firm performance, measured in standard deviations.

computed.notes: The primary outcome is a standardized business performance index aggregating weekly and monthly profits and revenues. Figure 3 and Table 1 report the average treatment effect as β = 0.04 standard deviations (p = 0.34, 95% CI = [-0.05, 0.15]). The paper states 'we are unable to reject the null hypothesis of no average treatment effect on firm revenues and profits' (page 3). The study used stratified randomization by quartiles of pre-treatment business performance and by gender, with 640 total participants (320 per arm). The effect size of 0.04 s.d. is interpreted as a standardized effect (Cohen's d). The paper reports heterogeneous effects by baseline performance: low performers had β = -0.08 s.d. (p = 0.01), high performers had β = 0.16 s.d. (p = 0.07), with a heterogeneous treatment effect of β = 0.23 s.d. (p = 0.01) between the two groups.
